header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: Diplomatic Pushback -- Disagreeing With People Who Know More Than You
lesson.menu_desc: Concessão e contra-argumento: conceder o que é verdade e NÃO largar a sua posição -- counterpoint, premise, anecdotal, rigor, to concede, to stand your ground -- diante de um conselho acadêmico de doutores -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 -- Diplomatic Pushback
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Aula 9 -- Diplomatic Pushback

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire. You present to the Academic Council on Thursday, and there is one thing I need you to understand before you walk in. Alan Prescott is going to take issue with your methodology. He will do it in the first four minutes, he will be perfectly polite about it, and he will say that thirty-eight percent is anecdotal. Now, here is what I have watched happen to three people before you. They hear a doctor question their evidence, and they fold. They say, you are absolutely right, I will redo the whole assessment. And the moment they say that, the room quietly decides that the culture work is optional, and we do not get it back for a year. So do not fold. And do not fight him either, because you will lose that, and you should lose it, because he has published on this and you have not. What you do instead is the only thing that works in that room. You concede the part of his point that is true, out loud and without flinching, and then you hold the part that is yours. The sample is small. Say so before he does. And then say what the thirty-eight percent actually is, because it is not a weak finding, Danielle, it is a finding about something else entirely, and you are the only person in that building who can see it.

Claire, it is Alan. You asked me for my honest expectations of Thursday, so here they are, and you are welcome to pass them on to her. I have read the assessment twice. The instrument is sound, the questions are well built, and the response rate is thirty-eight percent, which in my field would not survive a review. I intend to say so, and I would ask you not to treat that as hostility, because it is not. It is the only professional courtesy I have to offer somebody who is new. What I am genuinely trying to find out on Thursday is not whether her data is perfect. It is whether she knows where it is thin. Every consultant who has ever presented to this council has arrived certain, and certainty, in a room of researchers, reads as inexperience. So if she tells me that the number is small and then tells me why it is small and what it means anyway, I will listen to the rest of it with a great deal of interest. And if she simply agrees with everything I say in order to get out of the room, then I am afraid I will have learned something about her that I would rather not know in her first month.

items.situation: "Thirty-eight percent. In my field, that is not a finding. That is a rumor."
tips: Granted, the sample is small, and I would not defend it as representative. That said, the silence of the other sixty-two percent is itself the finding, and it is the one I would ask you to look at.
concede the number, then move the argument to what the number MEANS -- never defend the number

items.situation: "Your assessment is not peer-reviewed. Why should this council act on it?"
tips: While it is not peer-reviewed, it was never intended to be a study. It is a diagnostic, and I would defer to you entirely on the research question. What I am asking you to act on is an operational one.
concede the standard, then reframe the purpose -- a diagnostic and a study are not the same object

items.situation: "With respect, this is anecdotal. Four focus groups is not evidence."
tips: Even if we treat the focus groups as anecdotal, the pattern in them is consistent, and it is consistent with the one number we do trust. I take your point about weight. I would take issue with the word evidence.
concede the weight of the evidence, contest the category -- and do it without raising your voice

items.situation: "You have been here for six weeks. What could you possibly know about this faculty?"
tips: Very little, and that is precisely why I ran the assessment rather than arriving with a plan. I would defer to this council on what the faculty is. I would not defer to anyone on what the response rate says about how it feels to be asked.
concede the experience gap completely -- and then hold the one thing that is genuinely yours

items.situation: "Let me play devil's advocate. Suppose the culture here is perfectly healthy and your survey is simply badly timed."
tips: That is a fair premise to test, and it does not hold water on one point: a healthy faculty that is asked how it feels does not decline to answer at a rate of sixty-two percent. Everything else I would concede to you.
take the counterfactual seriously, then break it at exactly ONE point -- the strongest one

items.situation: "So what exactly are you asking this council to approve today?"
tips: Not the strategy. One thing: a second round of the instrument, designed with two members of this council, in March. Much as I would like to defend the thirty-eight percent, the honest answer is that I would rather replace it.
the close: concede your own weakness, and turn it into the ask
